narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your undergraduate studies and preparing for the LSAT. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 28.50, placement in the 4th decile for your Year 3 school grades, the 2nd decile for Year 1, and coming from a middle-income family, with plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 41%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your Year 3 decile ranking, which was somewhat better than average and suggested improving performance over time. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case somewhat, as it nudged the prediction slightly toward passing compared to lower-performing students. On the flip side, your Year 1 decile, being in the lower ranks well below the typical student's, pushed the model toward expecting failure, as early struggles often signal challenges ahead. Your LSAT score, lower than the average of around 37, and your middle-income background each contributed moderately toward the failure prediction, reflecting how these stack up against students who typically pass the bar. Being full-time was a small plus, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who boast higher deciles, GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 37, your profile showed some strengths in later academics but was weighed down by early and standardized test gaps. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many overcome similar starts through targeted growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record with additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing, and we believe in your future success.
